The logic for `isapprox` here is not correct yet. The way I plan to proceed is to implement specialized `norm` for structured matrices and then the fallback to the generic `isapprox` can be made much faster even if it does not fall back to element wise comparison.

-----------

Currently, `==` and `≈` fall back to generic methods. This PR adds special methods for structured matrix types. It also fixes an error in `bidiag.jl` where `==` fails:

```
julia> x = rand(3)
3-element Array{Float64,1}:
 0.7279081769922535
 0.8949580887222155
 0.7781661021218007

julia> Bu = Bidiagonal(x, zeros(2), 'U')
3×3 Bidiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.727908  0.0        ⋅      
  ⋅        0.894958  0.0     
  ⋅         ⋅        0.778166

julia> Bl = Bidiagonal(x, zeros(2), 'L')
3×3 Bidiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.727908   ⋅         ⋅      
 0.0       0.894958   ⋅      
  ⋅        0.0       0.778166

julia> Bu == Bl
false

julia> Matrix(Bu) == Matrix(Bl)
true
```

This PR

```
julia> x = rand(3)
3-element Array{Float64,1}:
 0.3172029052612273 
 0.8635761133663176 
 0.17013855660697486

julia> Bu = Bidiagonal(x, zeros(2), 'U')
3×3 Bidiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.317203  0.0        ⋅      
  ⋅        0.863576  0.0     
  ⋅         ⋅        0.170139

julia> Bl = Bidiagonal(x, zeros(2), 'L')
3×3 Bidiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.317203   ⋅         ⋅      
 0.0       0.863576   ⋅      
  ⋅        0.0       0.170139

julia> Bu == Bl
true

julia> Matrix(Bu) == Matrix(Bl)
true
```

------------------------------------------------------------

Some timings (these are pessimal cases):
v1.0
```
julia> D=Diagonal(zeros(1000));B=Bidiagonal(zeros(1000), zeros(999), 'U');T=Tridiagonal(zeros(999), zeros(1000), zeros(999))

julia> @time D == B
  0.636254 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true

julia> @time D == T
  0.008160 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true

julia> @time B == T
  0.629008 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true
```

This PR

```
julia> D=Diagonal(zeros(1000));B=Bidiagonal(zeros(1000), zeros(999), 'U');T=Tridiagonal(zeros(999), zeros(1000), zeros(999))

julia> @time D == B
  0.000007 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true

julia> @time D == T
  0.000005 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true

julia> @time B == T
  0.000012 seconds (4 allocations: 160 bytes)
true
```
-----------------------------------------
(Forgot to time `≈`):

v1.0

```
julia> @time D ≈ B
  1.253851 seconds (10 allocations: 24.047 KiB)
true

julia> @time D ≈ T
  0.029518 seconds (12 allocations: 39.953 KiB)
true

julia> @time B ≈ T
  0.669546 seconds (11 allocations: 32.016 KiB)
true
```

This PR

```
julia> @time D ≈ B
  0.000044 seconds (6 allocations: 8.109 KiB)
true

julia> @time D ≈ T
  0.000045 seconds (6 allocations: 8.109 KiB)
true

julia> @time B ≈ T
  0.000036 seconds (7 allocations: 16.047 KiB)
true
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29724/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":1,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29724/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2461270183","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/29724#issuecomment-2461270183","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/29724","id":2461270183,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6StACn","user":{"login":"mcognetta","id":6856636,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4NTY2MzY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6856636?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta","html_url":"https://github.com/mcognetta","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcognetta/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-07T04:00:08Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T04:00:08Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Yes, I will try. algebra"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2018-08-07T09:10:34Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:31:29Z","closed_at":"2021-06-27T19:57:54Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Multiplying a bidiagonal matrix times a triangular matrix fails as there is no matching rmul method.

MWE:
```
julia> A = Bidiagonal(rand(10, 10), :U)
julia> B = UpperTriangular(rand(10,10))
julia> B*A #this works
julia> A*B
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching rmul!(::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int64}, ::UpperTriangular{Float64,Array{Float64,2}})
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tests for this should also be added to LinearAlgebra/test/bidiag,jl around line 281. In that section, some sets of matrix types are evaluated but not in all orders so this method error doesn't show up.

I have two WIP PRs (#28343 JuliaLang/julia#28345) to specialize the output types of these sorts of matrix multiplications but this should be fixed first. There are a few small bugs that pop up when doing special combinations of matrices and types (especially triangular and bigint/bigfloats).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/548/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/548/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

library"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":19,"created_at":"2019-02-20T15:34:23Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:33:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, block diagonal matrices don't correctly support `istril` or `istriu`
```julia
julia> D = Diagonal([[1 2; 3 4], [5 6; 7 8]])
2×2 Diagonal{Array{Int64,2},Array{Array{Int64,2},1}}:
 [1 2; 3 4]      ⋅     
     ⋅       [5 6; 7 8]

julia> A = [1 2 0 0; 3 4 0 0; 0 0 5 6; 0 0 7 8] # full matrix of D
4×4 Array{Int64,2}:
 1  2  0  0
 3  4  0  0
 0  0  5  6
 0  0  7  8

julia> istriu(D)
true

julia> istriu(A)
false
```

These can be solved by adding

```julia
istril(D::Diagonal{<:Number}) = true
istril(D::Diagonal) = all(istril, D.diag)
```
etc.

Once this is fixed, what should the intended behavior of `tril(D, k)`, `triu(D, k)`, `diag(D, k)`, etc. be?
`tril(D, k)` can just recursively check the blocks but should we make `diag(D, k)` actually return the k-th super diagonal of the 'full' matrix?

I realize this is quite low on the priority list, but it seems like block diagonal matrices were hastily added and a lot of support is missing.

```


 This is trickier to fix than the other edge cases, as `tril!` and `triu!` try to retain the original objects from the `SymTridiagonal` matrix.

Take for example:

```julia

julia> x = rand(3)
3-element Array{Float64,1}:
 0.6930350330844504
 0.6468377367848175
 0.6064723615323844

julia> y = rand(2)
2-element Array{Float64,1}:
 0.02044578190049906
 0.32110679242111617

julia> S = SymTridiagonal(x, y)
3×3 SymTridiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.693035   0.0204458   ⋅ 
 0.0204458  0.646838   0.321107
  ⋅         0.321107   0.606472

julia> T = tril!(S, -1)
3×3 Tridiagonal{Float64,Array{Float64,1}}:
 0.0        0.0        ⋅ 
 0.0204458  0.0       0.0
  ⋅         0.321107  0.0

julia> T[2, 1] = 1
1

julia> y
2-element Array{Float64,1}:
 1.0
 0.32110679242111617
```

I tried to fix this by using views:

```julia 

function tril!(M::SymTridiagonal, k::Integer=0)
    n = length(M.dv)
    if !(-n - 1 <= k <= n - 1)
        throw(ArgumentError(string("the requested diagonal, $k, must be at least ",
            "$(-n - 1) and at most $(n - 1) in an $n-by-$n matrix")))
    elseif k < -1
        fill!(M.ev,0)
        fill!(M.dv,0)
        return Tridiagonal(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1],M.dv,copy(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1]))
    elseif k == -1
        fill!(M.dv,0)
        return Tridiagonal(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1],M.dv,zero(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1]))
    elseif k == 0
        return Tridiagonal(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1],M.dv,zero(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1]))
    elseif k >= 1
        return Tridiagonal(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1],M.dv,copy(@view M.ev[1:length(M.dv)-1]))
    end
end
```

But this fails as `Tridiagonal` cannot have two different vector types as off-diagonals. 
```julia
MethodError: no method matching LinearAlgebra.SymTridiagonal(::Vector{Float32}, ::SubArray{Float32, 1, Vector{Float32}, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}}, true})
```

Another way could be to pop the last element off of `ev` if it is the same length as `dv`.

For example, with:

```julia

function mytril!(M::SymTridiagonal, k::Integer=0)
    n = length(M.dv)
    if !(-n - 1 <= k <= n - 1)
        throw(ArgumentError(string("the requested diagonal, $k, must be at least ",
            "$(-n - 1) and at most $(n - 1) in an $n-by-$n matrix")))
    end

    if length(M.ev) == n
        pop!(M.ev)
    end
    
    if k < -1
        fill!(M.ev,0)
        fill!(M.dv,0)
        return Tridiagonal(M.ev,M.dv,copy(M.ev))
    elseif k == -1
        fill!(M.dv,0)
        return Tridiagonal(M.ev,M.dv,zero(M.ev))
    elseif k == 0
        return Tridiagonal(M.ev,M.dv,zero(M.ev))
    elseif k >= 1
        return Tridiagonal(M.ev,M.dv,copy(M.ev))
    end
end
```
We can do the following:

```julia
julia> x = rand(3)
3-element Vector{Float64}:
y 0.13991260012971518
 0.8075381450020083
 0.643030111871688

julia> y = rand(3)

julia> S = SymTridiagonal(x, y)

julia> tril!(S, -1)
ERROR: ArgumentError: cannot construct Tridiagonal from incompatible lengths of subdiagonal, diagonal and superdiagonal: (3, 3, 3)
Stacktrace:
 [1] Tridiagonal{Float64, Vector{Float64}}(dl::Vector{Float64}, d::Vector{Float64}, du::Vector{Float64})
   @ LinearAlgebra ~/github/julia-dev/usr/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/LinearAlgebra/src/tridiag.jl:492
 [2] Tridiagonal
   @ ~/github/julia-dev/usr/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/LinearAlgebra/src/tridiag.jl:531 [inlined]

julia> mytril!(S, -1)
3×3 Tridiagonal{Float64, Vector{Float64}}:

```

Does this seem reasonable? I think that JuliaLang/julia#42472 should probably still be submitted first (they cause silent errors, where as this one will just fail because `Tridiagonal`'s length check will fail) and these changes can come in a different PR if people think its the right way to go. It also has behavior that is correctly described by the docs, but counter to what would be expected, for `Triangular` matrices. Specifically, the underlying matrix `.data` is called directly by `nonzeros`, with no attempt to filter out non-zero values that appear in the wrong half of the matrix.

The docs state: 

```
  nonzeros(A)

  Return a vector of the structural nonzero values in sparse array A. This includes zeros that are explicitly stored in the sparse array. The returned vector points
  directly to the internal nonzero storage of A, and any modifications to the returned vector will mutate A as well. See rowvals and nzrange.
```

which is what the function does, but not what I think users would expect from the name (as they would expect it to only return non-structural-zeros that fall in the correct half).

See the following examples:
